customer.perspective: Non-expert consumer who does not know heart rate terminology

engineer.perspective: Software engineer validating data-to-text correctness and deterministic behavior

qae.perspective: QA engineer validating completeness, safety, and regression behavior

report_quality.perspective: Holistic report quality: outcome correctness, safety, audience fit, and usability

scoring_rules.hard_fail: RPT-001 or RPT-003 < 4 → sample fails regardless of other scores
scoring_rules.audience_gate: RPT-004 or RPT-005 < 3 → flagged as needs-revision-for-audience-fit

criteria.description: Plain language - no medical jargon (HRV, SDNN, RHR, VO2, parasympathetic, bpm in context)

scoring_guide.1: Multiple jargon terms appear with no explanation; text reads like a medical report
scoring_guide.2: One or two jargon terms slip through; most text is accessible
scoring_guide.3: No raw jargon but uses semi-technical phrasing that may confuse a layperson
scoring_guide.4: All language is plain; one minor phrasing could be simpler
scoring_guide.5: Entirely conversational, anyone could understand on first read

fail_examples: Your HRV dropped 15ms below your 7-day baseline
SDNN variance is elevated, parasympathetic tone is low
RHR 78bpm, VO2max trending down this week
pass_examples: Your body's recovery signal dipped a bit overnight
Heart rhythm patterns suggest a lighter day would help
Sleep score dropping due to low hours

criteria.description: Emotional safety - never blame the user for their metrics or behavior

scoring_guide.1: Directly blames or shames the user for poor metrics
scoring_guide.2: Implies fault through phrasing like 'you only' or 'you didn't'
scoring_guide.3: Neutral but misses the chance to reframe objectively
scoring_guide.4: Mostly objective framing with one borderline phrase
scoring_guide.5: Entirely objective, data-driven framing with no blame or guilt

fail_examples: You didn't sleep enough last night
You only got 4 hours of sleep last night
You should have gone to bed earlier
pass_examples: Sleep score dropping due to low hours
Recovery is lower today - rest is the best lever right now
Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.

criteria.description: Actionability - every recommendation says what to DO, not just what is wrong

scoring_guide.1: States a problem with no suggestion at all
scoring_guide.2: Vaguely hints at action but nothing concrete
scoring_guide.3: Provides a general direction but no specific action
scoring_guide.4: Gives a clear action with minor ambiguity
scoring_guide.5: Every recommendation includes a specific, doable action

fail_examples: Your recovery is low today
Stress levels are elevated
Sleep quality has been declining
pass_examples: Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.
Try a 10-minute walk to ease into the day
Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.

criteria.description: Greeting appropriateness - matches the time of day

scoring_guide.1: Greeting contradicts the time of day (Good morning at 10pm)
scoring_guide.2: Greeting is generic and ignores time completely
scoring_guide.3: Greeting is time-neutral but misses an opportunity to be warm
scoring_guide.4: Greeting matches time of day with minor awkwardness
scoring_guide.5: Greeting naturally matches the time of day and feels warm

fail_examples: Good morning! at 9:00 PM
Rise and shine! at 11:00 PM
Time to wind down at 7:00 AM
pass_examples: Good morning - here's how last night went
Evening check-in: winding down looks smart tonight
Afternoon update: solid day so far

criteria.description: Emoji and buddy mood coherence - mood indicator matches the overall tone of the text

scoring_guide.1: Happy emoji/mood paired with alarming or negative text
scoring_guide.2: Mood is mismatched in a noticeable way
scoring_guide.3: Mood is neutral and doesn't conflict but doesn't reinforce either
scoring_guide.4: Mood mostly matches with one minor inconsistency
scoring_guide.5: Mood perfectly reinforces the text tone

fail_examples: Cheerful buddy icon next to 'Recovery is critically low, rest immediately'
Sad mood indicator with 'Great day! You crushed your goals'
Celebratory emoji on a rest-mode warning
pass_examples: Calm buddy mood with 'Take it easy today, recovery is building'
Energetic mood with 'Strong recovery - push yourself today'
Neutral mood with 'Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.'

criteria.description: Goal motivation - goal text encourages progress, never guilts the user

scoring_guide.1: Goal text shames the user for not hitting targets
scoring_guide.2: Goal text implies disappointment or failure
scoring_guide.3: Goal text is factual but flat, no encouragement
scoring_guide.4: Goal text is encouraging with one minor guilt-adjacent phrase
scoring_guide.5: Goal text is motivating, forward-looking, and blame-free

fail_examples: You failed to hit your step goal again
Only 2,000 of 10,000 steps - not enough effort
You're being lazy, get moving
pass_examples: 3,200 steps down, 6,800 to go - a short walk gets you closer
Rest day adjusted your goal - even small movement counts
Solid start. Another 4,000 steps and you'll hit your target

criteria.description: Recovery framing - uses objective data framing, never subjective blame

scoring_guide.1: Recovery text directly blames user choices for poor recovery
scoring_guide.2: Recovery text implies user is at fault through word choice
scoring_guide.3: Recovery text is neutral but leans toward subjective framing
scoring_guide.4: Recovery text is mostly objective with one borderline phrase
scoring_guide.5: Recovery text is entirely data-driven and constructive

fail_examples: You wrecked your recovery by staying up late
Bad recovery because you didn't rest enough
Your poor choices led to low recovery
pass_examples: Recovery is lower today - sleep duration was the main factor
Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.
Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.

criteria.description: Stress sensitivity - stress-related text does not cause MORE stress

scoring_guide.1: Stress text is alarming and likely to increase user anxiety
scoring_guide.2: Stress text uses urgent language that could worry the user
scoring_guide.3: Stress text is factual but cold, misses reassurance
scoring_guide.4: Stress text is calming with one slightly anxious phrase
scoring_guide.5: Stress text is calming, normalizing, and provides a gentle next step

fail_examples: WARNING: Your stress levels are dangerously high!
Your body is under extreme stress, this is concerning
Stress has been critically elevated for 3 days straight
pass_examples: Stress patterns are a bit elevated - a few deep breaths can help
Your body's been working hard. A calm evening helps reset things
Stress is higher than usual. A short walk or quiet time helps

criteria.description: Consistency across pages - dashboard message doesn't contradict stress page or insights

scoring_guide.1: Dashboard says 'great day' while stress page says 'high stress warning'
scoring_guide.2: Noticeable contradiction between two pages
scoring_guide.3: Pages don't contradict but tell very different stories
scoring_guide.4: Pages are consistent with one minor tone mismatch
scoring_guide.5: All pages tell a coherent story with consistent tone and data

fail_examples: Dashboard: 'Excellent recovery!' / Stress: 'Your body is overwhelmed'
Insights: 'Great sleep trend' / Dashboard: 'Sleep has been poor'
Goals: 'Push hard today' / Recovery: 'Take it easy, recovery is low'
pass_examples: Dashboard: 'Solid recovery' / Stress: 'Manageable stress' / Goals: 'Moderate effort today'
Dashboard: 'Rest day' / Stress: 'Take it easy' / Goals: 'Adjusted goals for recovery'
All pages reflect improving trend with encouraging tone

criteria.description: First impression - would a non-expert understand what to do within 5 seconds?

scoring_guide.1: Text is confusing, dense, or requires domain knowledge to parse
scoring_guide.2: Text is somewhat clear but requires re-reading
scoring_guide.3: Main point is visible but action isn't immediately obvious
scoring_guide.4: Clear message and action with minor complexity
scoring_guide.5: Instantly clear what the situation is and what to do

fail_examples: Based on multi-day HRV trend analysis, parasympathetic reactivation suggests modified periodization
Composite readiness index factoring sleep, strain, and recovery yields a moderate recommendation
Your 7-day rolling SDNN average is within normal variance
pass_examples: Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.
Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.
Strong day ahead - your body is ready for a solid workout

criteria.description: Data-text consistency - if sleep=2h, text should reflect severe sleep deprivation

scoring_guide.1: Text completely contradicts the data (e.g. 'Great sleep!' when sleep=2h)
scoring_guide.2: Text understates or overstates the data significantly
scoring_guide.3: Text roughly matches data but misses severity or nuance
scoring_guide.4: Text accurately reflects data with one minor mismatch
scoring_guide.5: Text perfectly mirrors the severity and meaning of the underlying data

fail_examples: Sleep=2h: 'Solid night of rest, you're well recovered'
Steps=500: 'Great activity day, keep it up!'
Readiness=20: 'You're primed for a big workout'
pass_examples: Sleep=2h: 'Very low sleep last night - prioritize rest today'
Steps=500: 'Light movement day. Even a short walk adds up'
Readiness=20: 'Recovery is low. Take it easy and let your body reset'

criteria.description: Score-mode alignment - readiness score matches guidance mode (low score produces rest mode)

scoring_guide.1: Mode completely mismatches score (push mode at readiness=15)
scoring_guide.2: Mode is wrong by one level (moderate mode at readiness=15)
scoring_guide.3: Mode direction is correct but intensity is off
scoring_guide.4: Mode matches score with minor calibration issue
scoring_guide.5: Mode perfectly corresponds to the readiness score range

fail_examples: Readiness=15, mode=push: 'Time to go all out today!'
Readiness=90, mode=rest: 'Take a full rest day'
Readiness=30, mode=moderate: 'A solid moderate effort is great today'
pass_examples: Readiness=15, mode=rest: 'Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.'
Readiness=70, mode=moderate: 'Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.'
Readiness=90, mode=push: 'Strong recovery - your body is ready to push today'

criteria.description: Monotonic graduation - as metrics worsen day-over-day, text severity increases

scoring_guide.1: Text severity decreases while metrics worsen across days
scoring_guide.2: Text severity stays flat despite significant metric decline
scoring_guide.3: Text severity increases but not proportionally to metric decline
scoring_guide.4: Text severity tracks metric decline with minor lag
scoring_guide.5: Text severity smoothly and proportionally tracks metric changes

fail_examples: Day1 sleep=6h: 'Rest up' then Day3 sleep=3h: 'Looking good!'
Day1 stress=high: 'Take it easy' then Day5 stress=critical: 'Manageable stress today'
Declining readiness over 5 days but text stays 'Decent recovery' every day
pass_examples: Day1 sleep=6h: 'A bit less sleep' then Day3 sleep=3h: 'Very low sleep - rest is essential'
Gradually escalating concern as stress stays elevated across days
Recovery text shifts from 'moderate day' to 'take it easy' to 'full rest' as readiness drops

criteria.description: No stale recommendations - if day 5 is improving, don't show day 3's 'rest up' text

scoring_guide.1: Recommendations clearly reference a past state that no longer applies
scoring_guide.2: Recommendations lag behind current data by multiple days
scoring_guide.3: Recommendations are slightly behind current trajectory
scoring_guide.4: Recommendations match current data with slight delay in tone shift
scoring_guide.5: Recommendations precisely reflect the current day's data and trajectory

fail_examples: Day 5 readiness=80 but text says 'Still recovering, keep resting'
Metrics improved 3 days ago but text still warns about decline
Sleep normalized but text says 'Sleep has been poor recently'
pass_examples: Day 5 readiness=80: 'Recovery is bouncing back - moderate effort looks good'
Improving sleep trend: 'Sleep is trending up. Keep the momentum going'
After 3 bad days then 2 good: 'Nice rebound. Your body is responding well'

criteria.description: Number accuracy - step remaining = target minus current, sleep hours match input data

scoring_guide.1: Numbers in text are clearly wrong (math errors, wrong values)
scoring_guide.2: One number is incorrect or inconsistent with input
scoring_guide.3: Numbers are approximately correct but imprecise
scoring_guide.4: All numbers correct with minor rounding inconsistency
scoring_guide.5: All numbers are exactly correct and consistent with input data

fail_examples: Steps=3000, target=10000: 'Only 5,000 steps to go' (should be 7,000)
Sleep=5.5h displayed as 'about 7 hours of sleep'
Goal=8000 steps, current=6000: '4,000 remaining'
pass_examples: Steps=3000, target=10000: '7,000 steps to go - a walk gets you closer'
Sleep=5.5h: 'About 5 and a half hours of sleep last night'
Goal=8000, current=6000: '2,000 more steps to hit your goal'

criteria.description: Cross-page data consistency - same metric appears the same on all pages

scoring_guide.1: Same metric shows different values on different pages
scoring_guide.2: Metric interpretation conflicts between pages
scoring_guide.3: Values match but characterization differs (e.g. 'good' vs 'poor' for same value)
scoring_guide.4: Consistent values and interpretation with minor tone difference
scoring_guide.5: Identical values and consistent interpretation across all pages

fail_examples: Dashboard shows sleep=7h, insights page says sleep=5h
Dashboard calls recovery 'excellent', stress page calls it 'concerning'
Step count differs between goal card and activity summary
pass_examples: Sleep=6h shown consistently on dashboard, insights, and recovery sections
Recovery characterized as 'moderate' across all page references
Step count matches between goal progress and activity summary

criteria.description: Null handling - nil metrics produce graceful text, not crashes or blanks

scoring_guide.1: Nil metric causes blank text, 'nil', 'N/A', or visible error
scoring_guide.2: Nil metric produces awkward or broken sentence
scoring_guide.3: Nil metric produces generic placeholder that feels out of place
scoring_guide.4: Nil metric produces reasonable fallback text with minor awkwardness
scoring_guide.5: Nil metric produces natural, context-appropriate fallback text

fail_examples: Sleep=nil: '' (empty string)
HRV=nil: 'Your HRV is nil today'
Steps=nil: 'You took N/A steps'
pass_examples: Sleep=nil: 'No sleep data available yet - wear your watch tonight'
HRV=nil: 'Waiting for enough heart data to show your recovery trend'
Steps=nil: 'Step tracking starts once we get data from your watch'

criteria.description: Edge case text - extreme values (0 sleep, 200 RHR) produce sensible text

scoring_guide.1: Extreme value produces nonsensical or dangerous recommendation
scoring_guide.2: Extreme value produces text that ignores the severity
scoring_guide.3: Text acknowledges the extreme but response is generic
scoring_guide.4: Text correctly identifies the extreme with appropriate urgency
scoring_guide.5: Text handles the extreme gracefully, with appropriate concern and safe guidance

fail_examples: Sleep=0h: 'Decent night of sleep'
RHR=200: 'Heart rate is a little elevated today'
Steps=0, day 5 in a row: 'Keep up the activity!'
pass_examples: Sleep=0h: 'No sleep recorded. Rest is essential today - take it very easy'
RHR=200: 'Unusually high heart rate detected. If you feel unwell, consult a doctor'
Steps=0 for days: 'Activity has been very low. Even brief movement helps'

criteria.description: Mode-goal coherence - rest mode doesn't show ambitious step goals

scoring_guide.1: Rest mode paired with aggressive goals (10k step push)
scoring_guide.2: Mode and goal direction conflict noticeably
scoring_guide.3: Mode and goal don't conflict but aren't well aligned
scoring_guide.4: Mode and goal are aligned with minor inconsistency
scoring_guide.5: Mode and goal are perfectly coherent and mutually reinforcing

fail_examples: Mode=rest: 'Aim for 12,000 steps today!'
Mode=push: 'Take it easy, maybe skip the workout'
Mode=rest: 'Try to beat yesterday's step count'
pass_examples: Mode=rest: 'Adjusted goal: gentle movement. Even 2,000 steps count'
Mode=push: 'Strong day ahead - aim for your full step target'
Mode=moderate: 'Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.'

criteria.description: Determinism - same input always produces same output

scoring_guide.1: Same input produces completely different text across runs
scoring_guide.2: Same input produces noticeably different phrasing or recommendations
scoring_guide.3: Same input produces similar but not identical text
scoring_guide.4: Same input produces identical text with minor formatting variance
scoring_guide.5: Same input always produces byte-identical output

fail_examples: Run 1: 'Take it easy' / Run 2: 'Push hard today' for identical inputs
Same persona day produces different mode assignments across runs
Greeting changes between runs despite same time-of-day input
pass_examples: Identical inputs always produce identical greeting, mode, and recommendations
Re-running the same persona day produces byte-for-byte identical JSON
Deterministic engine with no random components

criteria.description: Coverage completeness - all pages have non-empty text for every field

scoring_guide.1: Multiple pages or fields are missing text entirely
scoring_guide.2: One page is missing text or several fields are empty
scoring_guide.3: All pages have text but one or two fields are suspiciously generic
scoring_guide.4: All fields populated with minor placeholder-like text in one spot
scoring_guide.5: Every page and field has meaningful, context-specific text

fail_examples: Dashboard greeting is empty, insights body is blank
Stress page has text but recovery section shows nothing
Goal card shows 'N/A' instead of actual goal text
pass_examples: All pages - dashboard, stress, insights, goals - have populated text
Every card, greeting, body, and recommendation field contains content
Even nil-data scenarios produce meaningful fallback text

criteria.description: No judgment language - no 'lazy', 'failure', 'should have', 'only got', 'not enough' when referring to the user

scoring_guide.1: Multiple judgment words present directed at the user
scoring_guide.2: One clear judgment word or phrase directed at the user
scoring_guide.3: No direct judgment but tone implies user fault
scoring_guide.4: Entirely objective with one borderline word choice
scoring_guide.5: Zero judgment language; all framing is objective and data-driven

fail_examples: You're being lazy, get moving
You only got 4 hours of sleep - that's not enough
You should have rested more yesterday, now recovery is poor
pass_examples: Sleep score dropping due to low hours
Recovery is lower today - rest is the best lever right now
Activity has been light. Even a short walk adds momentum

criteria.description: No raw metrics leaking - no 'HRV: 45ms', 'RHR: 78bpm' in coaching text

scoring_guide.1: Raw metric values with units appear in coaching text
scoring_guide.2: One raw metric value leaks into user-facing text
scoring_guide.3: No raw values but metric names appear without explanation
scoring_guide.4: All metrics are translated to plain language with one minor leak
scoring_guide.5: Zero raw metrics in coaching text; all data is translated to natural language

fail_examples: Your HRV: 45ms is below your 7-day average of 52ms
RHR: 78bpm, trending up from 72bpm last week
SDNN dropped to 38ms overnight
pass_examples: Your body's recovery signal is a bit lower than your recent average
Resting heart patterns suggest taking it easy today
Sleep score dropping due to low hours

criteria.description: Time-of-day correctness - greeting matches hour, nudges appropriate for time

scoring_guide.1: Greeting or nudge clearly wrong for the time (bedtime nudge at 7AM)
scoring_guide.2: One time-sensitive element is mismatched
scoring_guide.3: Time elements are generic rather than wrong
scoring_guide.4: All time elements correct with one slightly off timing
scoring_guide.5: Greeting and all nudges perfectly match the time of day

fail_examples: 8:00 AM: 'Time to wind down for bed'
11:00 PM: 'Get up and start moving!'
6:00 AM: 'Hope you had a great afternoon'
pass_examples: 8:00 AM: 'Good morning - here's how last night went'
2:00 PM: 'Afternoon check-in: staying on track'
9:00 PM: 'Evening wind-down: tomorrow starts with tonight's rest'

criteria.description: Accessibility text present - all captured fields have non-empty content

scoring_guide.1: Multiple fields return empty strings or whitespace-only content
scoring_guide.2: One field is empty or whitespace-only
scoring_guide.3: All fields have content but some are just punctuation or fragments
scoring_guide.4: All fields have meaningful content with one minor gap
scoring_guide.5: Every field has complete, readable, accessible text

fail_examples: Greeting field is '' (empty string)
Recovery body text is '.' (just punctuation)
Goal subtitle contains only whitespace
pass_examples: All fields contain at least one complete sentence
Greeting, body, subtitle, and recommendation fields all populated
Even low-data scenarios produce readable fallback content

criteria.description: Text length bounds - no text > 300 chars, no text < 5 chars (except nil-allowed fields)

scoring_guide.1: Multiple fields exceed 300 chars or are under 5 chars
scoring_guide.2: One field is clearly out of bounds
scoring_guide.3: All fields within bounds but one is borderline (295+ chars)
scoring_guide.4: All fields comfortably within bounds with good density
scoring_guide.5: All fields are well-sized: concise, complete, within bounds

fail_examples: Recovery body is 450 characters of rambling text
Greeting is 3 characters: 'Hi.'
Insight text is a 500-character paragraph
pass_examples: All text fields between 20 and 200 characters
Greeting is warm and brief (30-60 chars)
Body text is informative without being verbose (80-250 chars)

criteria.description: No duplicate text - adjacent sections don't repeat the same sentence

scoring_guide.1: Same sentence appears verbatim in multiple adjacent sections
scoring_guide.2: Near-duplicate sentences in adjacent sections
scoring_guide.3: Similar phrasing in adjacent sections but not identical
scoring_guide.4: All sections have unique text with minor thematic overlap
scoring_guide.5: Every section has distinctly different text with no repetition

fail_examples: Greeting: 'Take it easy today' / Body: 'Take it easy today, recovery is low'
Goal text and recovery text contain the same sentence verbatim
Dashboard and insights show identical recommendation text
pass_examples: Greeting sets the tone, body explains why, recommendation says what to do
Each section adds new information without repeating previous sections
Recovery and goals reference the same theme but with different wording

criteria.description: Journey regression - improving metrics produce improving text over days

scoring_guide.1: Text gets worse or stays alarming as metrics improve over days
scoring_guide.2: Text fails to acknowledge improvement for multiple days
scoring_guide.3: Text slowly catches up but lags behind actual improvement
scoring_guide.4: Text tracks improvement with one day of lag
scoring_guide.5: Text immediately and accurately reflects metric improvement trajectory

fail_examples: Sleep improving day 3-5 but text still says 'Sleep has been poor'
Readiness recovering but mode stays 'rest' for 3 extra days
Stress dropping but text keeps saying 'Elevated stress patterns'
pass_examples: Day 3 sleep improves: 'Sleep is trending up. Keep the momentum going'
Readiness climbing: text shifts from 'rest' to 'moderate effort looks good'
Stress normalizing: 'Stress patterns settling down - nice recovery'

criteria.description: Persona appropriateness - text doesn't assume fitness level user doesn't have

scoring_guide.1: Text assumes athlete-level fitness for a sedentary persona or vice versa
scoring_guide.2: Text makes one incorrect fitness assumption
scoring_guide.3: Text is generic enough to not offend but misses persona specifics
scoring_guide.4: Text mostly matches persona with one minor assumption
scoring_guide.5: Text perfectly calibrated to the persona's fitness level and context

fail_examples: SedentarySenior: 'Time for your daily 5K run!'
TeenAthlete: 'Consider starting light, like a 5-minute walk'
ObeseSedentary: 'Push for a personal best today!'
pass_examples: SedentarySenior: 'A gentle walk around the block is a great start'
TeenAthlete: 'Strong recovery - your body can handle a solid session'
ObeseSedentary: 'Every bit of movement counts. Start with what feels good'

criteria.description: Medical safety - no diagnostic claims, no treatment suggestions, includes disclaimers where appropriate

scoring_guide.1: Text makes diagnostic claims or prescribes treatment
scoring_guide.2: Text strongly implies a diagnosis or specific medical action
scoring_guide.3: Text is medically neutral but misses a disclaimer where one would help
scoring_guide.4: Text is safe with appropriate hedging and one minor gap
scoring_guide.5: Zero diagnostic claims, no treatment advice, appropriate disclaimers present

fail_examples: You may have sleep apnea based on your patterns
Take a beta-blocker if your heart rate stays this high
Your arrhythmia is getting worse
pass_examples: Unusually high heart rate detected. If you feel unwell, consult a doctor
Patterns suggest your body needs more rest. This is not medical advice
Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.

criteria.description: Trend narrative — when week data is available, text conveys trajectory from the past, not just a today snapshot

scoring_guide.1: Text gives only today's number with zero reference to how it got there
scoring_guide.2: Text hints at history with a vague word like 'recently' but no direction
scoring_guide.3: Text references the past but trajectory direction is unclear
scoring_guide.4: Text clearly names the trajectory with one minor phrasing gap
scoring_guide.5: Text grounds today's state in a clear week-long narrative the user can feel

fail_examples: Recovery is low today (no context of how it got there)
Sleep was 5 hours (was this the same all week?)
Stress is elevated (is it getting better or worse?)
pass_examples: Sleep has been building back up all week. Tonight keeps the streak going.
Recovery has been slipping over recent days. Rest is the right call today.
Stress patterns have been settling down since Wednesday. Keep the calm momentum.

criteria.description: Dual-audience layering — the same sentence works for a layman (plain outcome) and an expert (can infer data depth) without jargon being visible

scoring_guide.1: Text is either pure jargon (layman lost) or so stripped down the expert learns nothing
scoring_guide.2: Text serves one audience but clearly fails the other
scoring_guide.3: Text is safe for both but neither audience gets real value
scoring_guide.4: Both audiences served with one minor gap in depth or clarity
scoring_guide.5: Layman reads a clear human outcome; expert can sense the data story behind it

fail_examples: Rest. (layman: ok but why? expert: what data?)
Your 7-day SDNN rolling average has dropped below the 15th percentile baseline. (layman: lost)
Body stuff is happening. (expert: useless)
pass_examples: Recovery has been building steadily this week. A moderate effort suits you well today.
Sleep patterns have been consistent. Your body is running on a good rhythm right now.
Heart patterns have been quieter than usual lately. A lighter day works well today.

criteria.description: Achievement celebration — genuine wins (best sleep week, goal streak, strong recovery run) are surfaced with warmth when the data supports it

scoring_guide.1: A clear personal best or strong week goes completely unacknowledged
scoring_guide.2: A win is present in the data but text is neutral or negative
scoring_guide.3: Text is positive but generic, not tied to the specific achievement
scoring_guide.4: Text acknowledges the win with one missed opportunity for specificity
scoring_guide.5: Text names the achievement specifically and makes the user feel it

fail_examples: Best recovery week in 30 days but text says: 'Decent recovery. Moderate effort works today.'
5-day sleep streak but text says: 'Sleep was 7 hours last night.'
Hit step goal 6 days in a row but text ignores it entirely
pass_examples: Best recovery week in a month. Your body is responding really well to the pattern you've built.
Six days hitting your sleep target in a row. That kind of consistency is where change happens.
Strongest 5-day stretch this month. Whatever you did this week, it's working.

criteria.description: Data horizon signaling — text correctly communicates whether implications are drawn from today's data or the past week's trend

scoring_guide.1: Text makes week-level claims with only 1 day of data, or ignores week context entirely when 7 days are available
scoring_guide.2: Horizon is ambiguous in a way that would confuse a user reading carefully
scoring_guide.3: Horizon is technically correct but not communicated naturally
scoring_guide.4: Horizon is clear with one minor phrasing ambiguity
scoring_guide.5: Text precisely signals its data window: today's snapshot vs week trend vs recent pattern

fail_examples: Day 1 only: 'Your sleep has been declining all week' (no week exists yet)
Day 6: 'Sleep was 5 hours tonight' (ignores 5 nights of trend that drove the guidance)
'Recovery is low' with no signal of whether this is today or a pattern
pass_examples: Day 1: 'Sleep was lighter than usual last night. One good night resets a lot.'
Day 6: 'Sleep has been building back up over the past few days. Tonight matters.'
Day 3: 'Today's recovery is lower than your recent week average. Rest is the right call.'

criteria.description: Trend direction accuracy — when week data drives the message, the trending language matches the actual metric direction

scoring_guide.1: Text says 'improving' when metrics clearly declined over the week, or vice versa
scoring_guide.2: Trend direction is wrong for one metric but correct for another
scoring_guide.3: Trend direction is correct but magnitude is significantly off
scoring_guide.4: Trend direction and magnitude are correct with one minor overshoot
scoring_guide.5: Trend language precisely matches the actual slope and magnitude of the data

fail_examples: Readiness: 70, 65, 58, 50, 42 over 5 days — text says 'You've been improving this week'
Sleep: 5h, 6h, 7h, 7.5h, 8h — text says 'Sleep has been rough lately'
HRV flat within 2ms for 6 days — text says 'Strong upward trend in recovery signals'
pass_examples: Readiness declining: 'Recovery has been slipping over recent days. Prioritize rest tonight.'
Sleep improving: 'Sleep has been climbing all week. Keep the momentum going.'
Flat metrics: 'Things have been holding steady this week. Consistency is its own kind of progress.'

criteria.description: No hyphen as em dash — app text uses em dashes (—) or restructures sentences. A hyphen (-) surrounded by spaces is a formatting error, not a style choice.

scoring_guide.1: Multiple instances of ' - ' used as sentence connectors across coaching text
scoring_guide.2: One clear instance of hyphen-as-dash in a coaching text field
scoring_guide.3: No hyphen-as-dash in primary fields but one appears in a secondary field
scoring_guide.4: Clean formatting with one borderline case in a goal or nudge label
scoring_guide.5: Zero hyphen-as-dash anywhere. Em dashes (—) used correctly or sentences restructured.

fail_examples: Prioritize rest tonight - sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.
Recovery is lower today - rest is the best lever right now.
Decent recovery - a moderate effort works well today.
pass_examples: Prioritize rest tonight. Sleep is the biggest lever for recovery.
Recovery is lower today. Rest is the best lever right now.
Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.

criteria.description: Text freshness — text changes meaningfully across journey days. The same phrasing must not appear on days with meaningfully different metrics.

scoring_guide.1: Day 1 and Day 7 produce byte-identical hero messages despite very different metrics
scoring_guide.2: Text changes word-for-word in minor ways but the meaning is identical across days
scoring_guide.3: Text changes noticeably but one frozen phrase appears regardless of day state
scoring_guide.4: Text is fresh across most days with one reused phrase that is tolerable
scoring_guide.5: Every day produces text that could only have been written for that specific day and state

fail_examples: Day 1 readiness=72 and Day 7 readiness=28 both produce: 'Decent recovery. A moderate effort works well today.'
All 7 days start with 'Recovery is building.' regardless of whether it is
Hero message on day 2 and day 6 are identical despite one being a crash day
pass_examples: Day 1: 'Solid start. Your body is in a good rhythm.' / Day 7: 'Best recovery week in a month. Whatever you did, it worked.'
Crash day: 'Recovery took a hit today. Rest is the one lever that actually works right now.' / Recovery day: 'Bouncing back. Your body responded fast.'
Each day's text reflects the specific intersection of that day's metrics and the week's trajectory

criteria.description: Data correctness & internal consistency — all text and visuals accurately reflect the underlying data; no contradictions between sections

scoring_guide.1: Clear contradictions or incorrect claims — text says 'improving' when data is declining
scoring_guide.2: Significant mismatches in one section; loose phrasing that misleads about data state
scoring_guide.3: Mostly accurate with minor mismatches that don't truly mislead
scoring_guide.4: All claims accurate; only cosmetic inconsistencies in tone or word choice
scoring_guide.5: All text and visuals consistently match the data and each other; zero contradictions

fail_examples: Hero says 'strong recovery week' but readiness declined from 75 to 40 over 7 days
Graph shows RHR trending down but text says 'your RHR has been rising'
pass_examples: Readiness dropped 30 points and hero correctly reads 'Recovery has taken a hit — rest is the call today'
All sections — hero, banner, goals, nudges — reflect the same story from the same data

criteria.description: Goal alignment & appropriateness — recommended goals and daily moves match the user's situation, constraints, fitness level, and current risk

scoring_guide.1: Goals are inappropriate or unsafe — intense running goal for injured or overtrained user
scoring_guide.2: Goals are generic and partially misaligned with persona constraints or current data
scoring_guide.3: Goals are generic but not harmful or obviously unrealistic
scoring_guide.4: Goals are mostly tailored and realistic with one minor misalignment
scoring_guide.5: Goals are tailored, realistic, and clearly linked to the user's current state and constraints

fail_examples: Goal suggests 10,000 steps for a user with readiness=18 and 3h sleep
Sleep goal targets 9h for a new parent who structurally cannot sleep more than 5h
pass_examples: Overtrained user: 'Today's goal: gentle movement only. Even 1,500 steps counts.'
Sleep-deprived user: step goal auto-reduced, primary goal reframed as 'rest before 10pm'

criteria.description: Risk handling & safety — report never encourages unsafe behavior; appropriately escalates when risk is high; medical framing is cautious and non-diagnostic

scoring_guide.1: Encourages high-intensity activity when risk signals are severe; dangerous recommendation
scoring_guide.2: Under-emphasizes risk in a way that could lead to harm; missing escalation when warranted
scoring_guide.3: Some risk under-emphasized but no clearly dangerous recommendation
scoring_guide.4: All risk states correctly reflected; no unsafe nudges; escalation present when needed
scoring_guide.5: Risk correctly identified; no unsafe encouragement; calm escalation where appropriate; no diagnostic claims

fail_examples: Readiness=12, sleep=2h, stress=critical: 'Push yourself today — your body can handle it!'
RHR 40% above baseline for 3 days: 'heart rate looks a bit high, no action needed'
pass_examples: Readiness=12: 'Very low recovery. Rest is the only right call today.'
Sustained RHR elevation: 'Patterns like this are worth mentioning to your doctor if you feel unwell.'

criteria.description: Audience fit — layperson clarity: non-technical user can read the report, understand their status, and know what to do

scoring_guide.1: Relies heavily on jargon; a typical user cannot interpret or act on the report
scoring_guide.2: Some accessible language but key sections use unexplained terms or abstract advice
scoring_guide.3: Generally understandable; some jargon or dense text may confuse a non-expert
scoring_guide.4: Mostly plain language; 1-3 clear actions; one minor phrasing could be simpler
scoring_guide.5: A layperson instantly grasps status, why it matters, and next steps without technical knowledge

fail_examples: 'Your HRV RMSSD dropped 18ms below your 28-day baseline, indicating parasympathetic suppression'
Three paragraphs of data before any clear action or human outcome
pass_examples: 'Your heart had a tough night. A calm, easy day helps it reset.' + 'Try: short walk after dinner'
Main screen: status in one sentence, why in one sentence, do-this in one sentence

criteria.description: Audience fit — professional transparency: a health or technical professional can see how conclusions were reached and verify they are reasonable

scoring_guide.1: Conclusions are opaque; no data visible; appear to overstate what the inputs support
scoring_guide.2: Some data visible but traceability from data to recommendations is only partially clear
scoring_guide.3: Key inputs are named; professional can broadly verify but not fully audit
scoring_guide.4: Data signals visible and reasoning clearly stated; narrative doesn't overclaim
scoring_guide.5: Professional can inspect the rationale and verify narrative-data alignment

fail_examples: Report says 'recovery is low' with no indication of which signals drove it
Trend claim made with no supporting data visible anywhere
pass_examples: 'Recovery signal lower than usual — mainly driven by shorter sleep (5h vs typical 7h) and slightly elevated resting heart rate'
Detail view shows 7-day RHR trend, sleep hours, and HRV range

criteria.description: Progressive detail & explainability — users can drill into any element for a plain explanation; layered from summary to details without forcing complexity on casual users

scoring_guide.1: No drill-down; user is stuck at surface or dumped into raw graphs with no explanation
scoring_guide.2: Some detail available but navigation unclear or explanations skip plain-language step
scoring_guide.3: Detail path exists but uneven — some cards explain well, others don't
scoring_guide.4: Interactive elements discoverable; first expansion clear; second step leads to graphs
scoring_guide.5: Three clear layers: summary → plain 'why' → data graphs. Lay users stop at 1-2; professionals go to 3

fail_examples: Tapping 'Heart Mode: Caution' opens a raw HRV chart with no explanation
No tap targets — report is static with no drill-down available
pass_examples: Tap 'Caution' → plain one-sentence why → 'View your 7-day trend'
Every card has a chevron; first tap: plain sentence; second tap: graph

criteria.description: Graph clarity & text-graph alignment — visuals are interpretable by non-experts and consistent with the text narrative

scoring_guide.1: Graphs confusing, unlabeled, or clearly contradict the text
scoring_guide.2: Graphs correct but under-explained; text and graph tell different stories
scoring_guide.3: Graphs correct and labeled but require domain knowledge to interpret
scoring_guide.4: Graphs clear and mostly reinforce text; one minor mismatch
scoring_guide.5: Graphs simple, labeled in plain language, and clearly reinforce the written story

fail_examples: Text says 'RHR has been trending up' but graph shows a flat or declining line
Graph has no axis labels; values shown with no units or reference bands
pass_examples: Banded bar chart shows sleep hours with a green 'target' band; caption explains gap in plain English
RHR line with 'your typical range' shaded; text reads 'this week ran a little above your usual range'

criteria.description: Focus & prioritization — report surfaces the 1-2 most important issues immediately; secondary details stay in supporting roles

scoring_guide.1: Important issues buried or equal in visual weight to minor metrics
scoring_guide.2: Main issue present but shares too much space with secondary points
scoring_guide.3: Main issue visible but takes work to identify; too many equally-weighted items
scoring_guide.4: Clear primary issue highlighted with secondary context subordinated
scoring_guide.5: User immediately sees the top 1-2 issues; everything else is clearly secondary

fail_examples: Sleep debt of 3h listed 5th after step count, active calories, HRV, and zone breakdown
Bad day with readiness=15 shows 8 different cards at equal visual weight
pass_examples: Sleep debt leads the report on a bad night; steps and other metrics clearly secondary
Crash day: one large card 'Rest is the call today'; everything else collapses

criteria.description: Tone, empathy & motivation — message is emotionally appropriate, supportive, and motivating; avoids blame; supports autonomy

scoring_guide.1: Critical, shaming, or dismissive tone; likely to make users feel worse or defensive
scoring_guide.2: Neutral but cold; tone doesn't match the user's situation
scoring_guide.3: Neutral, not harmful, but generic and impersonal
scoring_guide.4: Warm and respectful; one missed opportunity for empathy
scoring_guide.5: Warm, respectful, motivating; acknowledges effort; hopeful and realistic; invites rather than commands

fail_examples: Crashed day: 'You have not met your goals this week. Try harder tomorrow.'
Commands: 'You must sleep 8 hours. You must reduce stress.'
pass_examples: Crashed day: 'Rough week — your body worked hard. Rest today is doing real work, even if it doesn't feel like it.'
'A short walk could help, even just around the block.' not 'You need to walk.'

criteria.description: Brevity & cognitive load — report is concise enough to be usable when tired or stressed; on bad days doesn't pile on

scoring_guide.1: Overly long, dense, or cluttered; likely to overwhelm or be skipped
scoring_guide.2: Noticeably wordy; bad-day reports list too many negatives
scoring_guide.3: A bit wordy but digestible with effort
scoring_guide.4: Concise with one section that could be trimmed
scoring_guide.5: Concise and focused; 1-3 main points; bad days get shorter not longer reports

fail_examples: Bad day: 8 separate issues at equal weight over 4 scrollable cards
Same insight repeated in hero, banner, and goals without adding new information
pass_examples: Bad day: one card, one action, one sentence of context
Guidance text: 2 sentences max; action item: 1 concrete thing

criteria.description: Breathing feature coherence — breathing usage reflected correctly; no praise for habits that don't exist; no overclaiming

scoring_guide.1: Praises breathing habit for a user who hasn't used it; claims breathing always fixes stress
scoring_guide.2: Some mismatch or overclaiming about breathing benefits
scoring_guide.3: Technically correct but messaging doesn't match usage patterns
scoring_guide.4: Messages align with actual behavior; correlation claims appropriately hedged
scoring_guide.5: Usage, messaging, and correlation claims coherent and cautious; goals sized for actual habit

fail_examples: User has never used breathing: 'Great job keeping up your breathing practice this week!'
'Breathing always reduces your stress — it's the single best tool you have'
pass_examples: New user: 'Breathing exercises can help — even 2 minutes makes a difference for some people'
Regular user: 'On days you've used breathing, your calm signal tends to recover faster'
